Title: Sensitive secrets (DB password, API key, TOTP setup key) copied to the global pasteboard with no expiry or local-only flag
Description: Copy actions wrote DB passwords, freshly created API keys and the TOTP setup secret to UIPasteboard.general as a plain string with no expirationDate / .localOnly.
Impact: Any co-resident app (with iOS 16 paste prompt) or a Handoff-paired device could read the secret; it persisted indefinitely. TOTP secret leak permanently defeats 2FA; API key is a long-lived scoped credential.
AttackerModel: on-device malware / user's own Universal-Clipboard-paired devices
Remediation: Added Clipboard.copySecret() using setItems with .localOnly + 60s .expirationDate; routed the three secret copies through it.

Title: No app-switcher privacy overlay; sensitive screens captured in the snapshot on backgrounding
Description: scenePhase handler never covered the UI when leaving the foreground, so the task-switcher snapshot captured console output, server IP:port and one-time-revealed secrets.
Impact: Anyone with brief physical access to an unlocked/handed-over device can read sensitive content from the app-switcher thumbnail without opening the app.
Remediation: Added an opaque branded PrivacyCurtain overlay shown whenever scenePhase != .active.

Title: App-lock does not re-engage when returning from background
Description: The Face ID app-lock ran only on cold start; backgrounding a signed-in session and returning resumed straight into content with no re-auth.
Impact: An attacker with the unlocked device can resume a signed-in privileged session even with the app-lock enabled, defeating its purpose.
Remediation: Set phase=.locked on scenePhase==.background when the lock is enabled; added an always-available Sign out escape on the lock screen.

Title: Authenticated API responses used the default shared on-disk URLCache
Description: APIClient used URLSession.shared whose default URLCache can persist authenticated JSON responses to the app container on disk.
Impact: Cached sensitive responses readable with filesystem access (jailbreak / unencrypted backup at rest after first unlock).
Remediation: Default to an ephemeral URLSession (in-memory cache, no persisted cookies/credentials, reloadIgnoringLocalCacheData).

Title: No certificate or public-key pinning for the API (REST) or Socket.IO transport
Description: TLS validation is intact (system default, no weakening) but there is no SPKI/certificate pinning, so any CA-trusted cert (incl. a maliciously/coerced-issued or user-installed root) is accepted.
Impact: An attacker able to present a trusted cert (rogue CA, MDM/user-installed root, corporate proxy) could MITM the token-bearing channel. Requires defeating standard TLS first.
AttackerModel: network MITM with a trusted cert
Remediation: Add SPKI pinning for the API + Socket.IO origins with backup pins and a documented rotation process; fail closed on mismatch (never .useCredential unconditionally). Architectural — needs ops support for cert rotation.

Title: Persisted apiOrigin override could redirect all token-bearing traffic
Description: AppConfig read an apiOrigin/webOrigin override from UserDefaults at launch. The in-app UI to set it was removed, leaving a dormant redirect vector for anyone able to write the app's sandboxed UserDefaults.
Impact: An attacker with sandbox write (jailbreak / tampered backup restore) could repoint all REST + Socket.IO traffic (with the Bearer token) to an attacker host.
Remediation: Gated the override read behind #if DEBUG; release always uses the baked-in Info.plist origin / fallback.

Title: Biometric app-lock failed open when the policy could not be evaluated
Description: authenticate() returned true (unlocked) when canEvaluatePolicy was false.
Impact: An attacker who could make biometrics/passcode unevaluable would bypass the soft lock. Low impact: the lock is a content-hiding gate; tokens are separately Keychain-protected.
Remediation: Return false (stay locked) on evaluation failure; safe because the lock screen now has a Sign out escape (AUTH-2).

Title: App-lock is a UI-phase gate only, not bound to a Keychain access-control item
Description: The biometric lock is a SwiftUI phase gate; the refresh token is stored AfterFirstUnlockThisDeviceOnly without SecAccessControl biometry binding, so on an instrumented device the gate is bypassable and the token still readable.
Impact: On a jailbroken/instrumented device the lock provides no real boundary. Documented in-code as privacy-only, not a security boundary.
Remediation: If a real boundary is desired, store the refresh token under SecAccessControl(.biometryCurrentSet/.userPresence) and supply an LAContext on read. Architectural; risks lockout on biometric change — needs UX for re-enrol/relogin.

Title: WebLink.open passed a server-controlled URL to UIApplication.open without scheme validation
Description: Signed file-download URLs returned by the backend were opened directly; a malicious/compromised backend could return a non-web scheme to launch another app.
Impact: A compromised backend could trigger opening of arbitrary URL schemes (deep links, tel:, etc.).
Remediation: Validate the resolved scheme is https (http allowed only in DEBUG) before calling UIApplication.open.

Title: No committed Package.resolved lockfile; SocketIO pinned with a floating from: range
Description: SocketIO (which transitively pulls Starscream) is pinned as from: 16.1.0 and Package.resolved is gitignored, so the exact resolved versions of the privileged build are not locked or reviewed.
Impact: Silent version drift / a compromised or vulnerable transitive release could enter the build unreviewed.
Remediation: Pin SocketIO to an exact tag in project.yml, commit a resolved lockfile (CI step, since XcodeGen regenerates the xcodeproj), and add dependency review to CI. Verify the resolved Starscream version against advisories.

Title: Distribution .p12 import passphrase hardcoded in the CI workflow
Description: P12_PASSWORD: refxci is committed in the workflow. It protects the distribution .p12, which itself is a GitHub secret (not in the repo), so the password alone is not sufficient to sign.
Impact: Defense-in-depth weakness: the passphrase is public in git history. Combined with any leak of the .p12 secret it would allow signing as the team.
Remediation: Move the passphrase to a GitHub Actions secret and rotate the distribution certificate/passphrase (the current value is effectively public; deleting from HEAD does not purge git history).

Title: Refresh-token single-flight is per-instance; the background task builds a second AuthStore that can double-spend the rotating refresh token
Description: BGAppRefreshTask constructs its own AuthStore with an independent single-flight guard. If a background refresh and a foreground refresh overlap, both may POST /auth/refresh with the same rotating token; the reused token can trip server-side family-reuse revocation.
Impact: Rare spurious full-session logout (user must re-authenticate). Not a credential-theft vector. Likelihood low because BG tasks run while backgrounded.
Remediation: Share one AuthStore (and single-flight) across foreground + background, or coordinate refresh via a process-wide mutex keyed on the token, and re-read the token from Keychain after refresh. Touches the rotation flow — verify before changing.

Title: localhost cleartext ATS exception ships in the release Info.plist
Description: An NSExceptionDomains entry permits cleartext http to localhost (previously incl. subdomains) in all builds.
Impact: Loopback-only; not remotely exploitable. Hardening only.
Remediation: Removed NSIncludesSubdomains; localhost http remains for local DEBUG panels. Consider a Debug-only Info.plist to drop the exception from release entirely.

Title: Server name + state disclosed on the lock screen via local notifications / Live Activity
Description: Outage notifications and the Live Activity show the server name and state on the lock screen. No secrets (IPs, tokens) are exposed.
Impact: Minor metadata disclosure to someone viewing the locked screen.
Remediation: Accepted as intended UX. Optional: generic notification titles / redact Live Activity when locked if server names are considered sensitive.

Title: Custom URL scheme refxapp:// declared but never handled
Description: The scheme is registered (widget deep link) but the app has no onOpenURL/open-URL handler, so no action path exists.
Impact: No exploit. Note for future deep-link work.
Remediation: No action. If routing is added, validate host/path, require in-app confirmation for any action, and prefer universal links over the custom scheme.

Title: App Store provisioning profiles committed to the repo (ci/*.mobileprovision)
Description: Provisioning profiles are committed for the Mac-free CI pipeline. A profile lists team id, app id, entitlements and (for dev profiles) device UDIDs, but contains no private key.
Impact: Not a vulnerability; minor metadata exposure / staleness hygiene.
Remediation: Optional: generate profiles at CI time via the App Store Connect API the workflow already uses, instead of committing them.
